items.title: Center before covariance
items.avoid: Passing raw feature columns into manual product-sum formulas.
items.prefer: Subtract means per column, then compute (X̃ᵀ X̃)/(n - 1) or use `np.cov` / `DataFrame.cov`.
items.why: Centering isolates co-movement around means—the definition of covariance.
items.example: X_centered = X - X.mean(axis=0); C = X_centered.T @ X_centered / (n - 1).

items.title: Choose covariance vs correlation deliberately
items.avoid: Feeding a correlation matrix into code that expects a covariance matrix (or vice versa).
items.prefer: Document which matrix your algorithm needs; convert explicitly with outer(std, std) when required.
items.why: Mixing them changes PCA scaling, regularization, and probabilistic interpretations.
items.example: sklearn PCA: `whiten=True` on unstandardized data uses Σ; standardize first if you want correlation-style geometry.

items.title: Inspect scatter plots for linearity
items.avoid: Relying solely on a single Pearson r value for feature relationships.
items.prefer: Pair plots or scatter charts for suspicious pairs; consider rank correlation when monotonic but non-linear.
items.why: Pearson r can hide strong non-linear structure that matters for modeling.
items.example: Before dropping a feature with r = 0.05, plot it against the target—a quadratic pattern may still be predictive.

items.title: Handle constant or near-constant columns
items.avoid: Computing correlation when a column has zero sample variance.
items.prefer: Drop constant features or guard division; treat undefined correlations as missing, not zero.
items.why: σ = 0 makes ρ undefined; libraries may return NaN or emit warnings.
